title: Archidendron multifoliolatum
intro: Archidendron multifoliolatum, also known as the white flower tree or five-leafed archidendron, is a species of flowering plant in the family Fabaceae. The plant has been traditionally used in Southeast Asian medicine for its various health benefits, including anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties, which are thought to aid in reducing fever, relieving pain, and protecting against infections. The bioactive constituents of Archidendron multifoliolatum include flavonoids, alkaloids, and phenolic acids, such as quercetin and kaempferol, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, Archidendron multifoliolatum is commonly prepared as a decoction, tincture, or infusion, and is often used to treat a range of ailments, including fever, cough, and respiratory infections.
taxonomy: Archidendron multifoliolatum is a species of flowering plant in the pea family, Fabaceae. It belongs to the genus Archidendron, which is a group of tropical trees and shrubs. The plant is native to Australia and New Guinea, and is also found in other parts of the world where the climate is warm and humid. Archidendron multifoliolatum is classified as a member of the subfamily Faboideae, which includes a wide range of legumes. The specific classification of the plant is Fabaceae, Faboideae, Desmodieae, Archidendron.
uses: Archidendron multifoliolatum, a plant native to tropical regions, has been used in traditional medicine for various purposes.
